provenance.source_corpus: Elucidating carbon sources driving microbial metabolism during oil sands reclamation

nodes.name: inputs of more modern, more bioavailable organic carbon sources

source_spans.text: Specifically, we investigated whether inputs of more modern, more bioavailable organic carbon sources from the surface environment were driving increased microbial activity and stimulating H2S production.

Specifically, we investigated whether inputs of more modern, more bioavailable organic carbon sources from the surface environment were driving increased microbial activity and stimulating H2S production.

Since there is evidence that much of the microbial activity within the sand cap is related to sulphur cycling ( Reid and Warren, 2016), these observations imply that these inputs of more modern carbon can greatly contribute to the generation of metabolites such as H2S that represent a potential management hazard at this site.

nodes.name: generation of metabolites such as H2S

source_spans.text: Since there is evidence that much of the microbial activity within the sand cap is related to sulphur cycling ( Reid and Warren, 2016), these observations imply that these inputs of more modern carbon can greatly contribute to the generation of metabolites such as H2S that represent a potential management hazard at this site.

nodes.name: inputs of younger, more bioavailable carbon

source_spans.text: The results of this study demonstrate that the impact of inputs of younger, more bioavailable carbon to geochemically reactive zones can result in stimulation of microbial biogeochemical cycling.
The results of this study demonstrate that the impact of inputs of younger, more bioavailable carbon to geochemically reactive zones can result in stimulation of microbial biogeochemical cycling.

nodes.name: utilization of this more modern carbon

source_spans.text: Correlation between cellular abundance estimates based on PLFA concentrations and the D14CPLFA indicated that the utilization of this more modern carbon was stimulating the microbial community at depth.

Correlation between cellular abundance estimates based on PLFA concentrations and the D14CPLFA indicated that the utilization of this more modern carbon was stimulating the microbial community at depth.
